Once China's largest developer and now a bellwether for the nation's struggles to ease broader property woes, Vanke plunged in credit and stock markets this week. The builder's local notes extended their declines Wednesday, with its bond due in May 2028 dropping as much as 29 yuan to 65 yuan…"

November 27 - Reuters: "China's top economic-planning agency has warned over the risk of a bubble forming in humanoid robotics, in a rare official expression of concern about the booming sector. 'Frontier industries have long grappled with the challenge of balancing the speed of growth against the risk of bubbles - an issue now confronting the humanoid robot sector as well,' Li Chao, spokeswoman of the National Development and Reform Commission, said… More than 150 makers of humanoid robots are operating in China and their number is still rising, Li said."

Europe Watch:

November 26 - Bloomberg (Philip Aldrick, Alex Wickham, Ellen Milligan, and Lucy White): "Chancellor of the Exchequer Rachel Reeves did enough in her budget to help her and Prime Minister Keir Starmer survive, building back a vital fiscal buffer and expanding welfare. Investors reacted positively. So did the rank-and-file Labour lawmakers who have frustrated their past spending plans. But on both fronts, there were few signs that the budget, with its £1.6 trillion ($2.1 trillion) of government spending, would by itself brighten the country's fortunes… The plan offered little on growth - the government's 'No. 1 mission' - and no meaningful tax reform. Nor did it provide the sort of grand vision Labour Members of Parliament have said Starmer needs to demonstrate to regain public support from Nigel Farage's populist Reform UK."

Japan Watch:

November 26 - Bloomberg (Alastair Marsh and Naureen S Malik): "Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi's government plans to issue more new bonds to fund its economic package than the corresponding amount last year… Takaichi's extra budget will be financed by additional bond issuance of at least ¥11.5 trillion ($73.5bn), according to… people… The newly added debt load is considerably larger than the ¥6.7 trillion issuance needed to fund former Prime Minister Shigeru Ishiba's set of economic measures a year ago."

Emerging Market Watch:

November 27 - Bloomberg (Juan Pablo Spinetto): "As financial scandals go, this one has it all: a flamboyant, politically connected executive taking on Brazil's financial titans with a fast-growing bank built on aggressive tactics and exotic financial engineering; a spectacular collapse amid billion-dollar fraud allegations; and an airport arrest just as the businessman was about to leave the country on his private jet… The downfall of Daniel Vorcaro and his Banco Master is now rippling across Brazil's business and banking circles, the latest major corporate implosion to shake Latin America's largest economy… The bank's liquidation has prompted the biggest intervention in the history of Brazil's financial markets. The Banco Master debacle caps a turbulent year for Brazilian companies. Earlier this month, telecom operator Oi SA was declared bankrupt and placed into liquidation… In September, waste-management firm Ambipar Participações e Empreendimentos SA unexpectedly entered a process akin to default… Around the same time, Braskem SA, Latin America's top petrochemical producer, hired advisers to shore up its capital structure… And there's the case of Raízen SA, the biofuels powerhouse turned penny stock. The list goes on…"

November 27 - Financial Times (Chris Kay): "India's economy expanded much faster than expected in the latest quarter, buoyed by robust consumer spending and growth in manufacturing and services that outweighed the hit to exports from US tariffs. GDP rose 8.2% year-on-year in the July to September quarter… That overshot a 7.3% forecast among economists… and beat the 7.8% rate in the previous quarter."

Leveraged Speculation Watch:

November 28 - Bloomberg (Greg Ritchie): "Hedge funds are pushing back against Bank of England proposals intended to limit risk taking among UK bond traders, arguing the policies would hurt liquidity and the market's global attractiveness. The Alternative Investment Management Association and the Managed Funds Association - the two largest industry groups representing hedge funds - do not support plans for so-called minimum haircuts on gilt repurchase agreements… Such a policy would effectively cap the amount of cash investors can borrow using gilts as collateral, limiting the ability of funds to amass leverage."

November 22 - Bloomberg (Georgie McKay): "Bill Ackman lit the fire and Bill Pulte supercharged it. Their influence helped drive retail traders to Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac, whose shares have soared more than 500% since Donald Trump's election a year ago. But now, as equity markets are gripped by volatility and crypto assets suffer their worst rout in years, those same investors are fleeing… Ackman, a billionaire hedge fund manager, sent out a social media post this week blaming forced liquidations and margin calls in the cryptocurrency market for the sagging prices on the mortgage giants."

Social, Political, Environmental, and Cybersecurity Instability Watch:

November 22 - Axios (Ben Geman): "UN climate talks in Brazil are wrapping up Saturday with a push to help move nations away from fossil fuels, but the deal lacks explicit mention of the CO2-spewing energy sources. The compromise underscores the Paris Agreement's limited ability to move from aspiration to stronger real-world action. It's a disappointment for nations and advocates that want an explicit 'roadmap' away from fossil fuels - coal, oil and gas. But it prevented collapse of the talks that had already extended beyond Friday's scheduled close."

November 27 - Reuters (Sybille de La Hamaide): "The bird flu virus that has been spreading among wild birds, poultry and mammals could lead to a pandemic worse than COVID-19 if it mutates to transmit between humans, the head of France's Institut Pasteur respiratory infections centre said. The highly pathogenic avian influenza, commonly called bird flu, has led to the culling of hundreds of millions of birds in the past few years, disrupting food supplies and driving up prices, though human infections remain rare."

November 25 - Bloomberg: "The real costs of climate change are rising, potentially faster than we're spending money on solutions. Insurers shelled out $1.4 trillion last year for climate-related damages, according to data from Bloomberg Intelligence. That is more than double the level of just a decade ago."

November 25 - Bloomberg (James Stavridis): "If you ask the average American, 'where does the internet come from?' the answer you would most likely get would be from space, via satellites. Wrong. The vast majority of information that flows across the tens of billions of devices connected to the internet comes from the sea. Around 500 fiber-optic undersea cables carry more than 95% of all internet data, strung like 19th-century telegraph cables under the oceans. And they are very vulnerable. While the cables are reasonably sturdy… it is possible to damage them. First, they can be vulnerable to natural disasters… A second threat is man-made: from terrorists, anarchists, luddites who hate the internet and other random agitators. While such actors can't really get at the seafloor cables, they can attempt to disrupt the connections at the water's edge, or use cybertools to hamper the cable operations."

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Galicia Q3 Earnings: A Tough Quarter, A Clean Reset, And A Market Already Looking Ahead
data.description:
Every time I sit down to analyze Galicia (GGAL), something happens to me that I already recognize, and it's that I end up reading much more than a balance sheet. It is as if the bank were an emotional mirror of the country. It seems that when the macro vibrates, GGAL vibrates; when it calms down, GGAL breathes; and when something goes wrong, GGAL is the first to show it in price.

While in June I was writing from an almost optimistic place, with the energy of a bank that had just absorbed HSBC and was expanding scale at exactly the same time, only a month later I was explaining how the whole banking system had been caught up in rates jumping from one day to the next and a liquidity that had become uncomfortable. Same bank, same story... different climate.

And today, after watching Q3 and seeing how the country risk fell without transition from the almost 1500 bps following the victory of the opposition in the provincial elections in the Province of Buenos Aires, to the 600 bps area following the mid-term elections, I have the feeling that the balance sheets have aged in a matter of days. GGAL published a hard number, yes, with margin pressure, higher uncollectibility, and an integration cost that weighed heavily. But I see that the market reacted as if it had read another document.

And that, for me, is the most interesting thing about GGAL: it's a bank that lives in the center of the Argentine pendulum. Absorbs macro shocks as few, but also amplifies any stability signal. And when the signal changes suddenly, as expectations changed after the elections, the stock translates it much faster than accounting statements.

Therefore, even with a clearly negative quarter, I do not find arguments to abandon the thesis. I had already moved from a buy in June to a Hold for the entire banking sector back in July. The photo of Q3 was hard, but the film that opened after runs on another lane. And that’s why my posture is a Hold with positive bias. Because this time, like so many others in Argentina, the signal that came after ended up being stronger than the quarter we just read.

The quarter to be spent

Let us now turn to the results of Q3. As I mentioned earlier, the quarter was very negative, as we can see from the numbers. The net loss of AR$87.7B is the first number that appears in the presentation of Q3, and I think there is no way to soften it. ROE stood at -4.7%, ROA at -0.8%, and the bank unit itself ended with AR$103.9B of negative results. Any reader who does not follow closely the dynamics of the Argentine system might think that this is a serious signal. But when you take the trouble to separate where the real blow is and where the operating normality is, the story begins to sort itself out.

The explanation given by GGAL was straightforward, and it's that the combination of higher reserve requirements and a more expensive funding cost squeezed the financial margin in a quarter when no one in the system managed to escape that dynamic. And that point is important, because this was not a GGAL problem, but a liquidity and rate shock that affected the entire system, as I have been warning since July.

Another point I must highlight is that the integration of HSBC, had an extraordinary weight in the line of results, which resulted in AR$105.3B in non-recurring expenses. That figure is enormous, yes, but it is the figure of absorbing an entire entity within another, in a country where the operational structure always has an additional complexity. It is not a number you project forward, but the cost of a strategic decision that has already been made.

The most interesting thing, for me, is when you look at the other side of the income statement. GGAL did not shrink through the quarter, as we see that deposits exceeded AR$22.9T and the credit portfolio advanced to AR$17.4T. That is, it lost money in a one-off period but did not lose its scale or operational capacity. And that, in a bank, and especially from Argentina, says much more than any isolated figure.

The disconnect between balance sheet and market

What caught my attention was not the result, but what happened next. Yesterday the results of Q3 were published, and today the stock rose. And it rose strongly, which leads me to think that the market decided that that outcome is part of the past.

But when I analyze in context, I have no doubt that the explanation lies in something that doesn't appear in any accounting statement, and it's the macro signals of these days. When the political climate ceases to pressure the country risk and the Argentine premium moves to levels that had not been seen for a long time, equity does not wait for the balance sheets to start improving, but directly advances the reading. In this sense, Galicia again behaves like the thermometer of the Argentine macro that I mentioned at the beginning of the article: it is the first to feel the cold, but it is also the first to show it when the temperature rises.

That is why, if you stay only with the loss, you see a weak quarter, but if you look at the reaction, you understand that the balance was not determinant. And for me, that’s the difference between reading accounting and reading financial climate.

The actual cost of the quarter

Sometimes I find it interesting to split the result of Q3 into two layers. The first layer I see is loss, provisions, margin squeeze, and extraordinary expenses. While the second one, which is the one that interests me most, is the one that shows that business continued to work, and today it sits in a context where the macro is quite different (for good) from the one that the bank faced during the quarter.

When I review Q3 in detail, I find a margin compressed by regulations that, in a normal context, would have moved otherwise. But I also find an irregularity that advanced, but remains at a manageable level for the system, even with a contracting quarter in the real economy. All this explains the loss, but it also explains why I do not see these results as a permanent deterioration.

What was exceptional was the cost of integrating HSBC. The rest was macro. What remains, which is pure banking business, stays where it should be.

The comparison that matters: ROE and P/B

When I compare the ROE and the P/B with the other banks listed on the NYSE, I find something that for me ends the reading. Despite Q3, GGAL still has the highest ROE TTM of all comparable Argentine banks, around 20.9%, far above BBVA Argentina (BBAR), Supervielle (SUPV) and Banco Macro (BMA), and that’s a sign that doesn’t deteriorate for just one quarter.

The same thing happens when I look at the P/B TTM, which is around 1.63x and reflects that, even in the months of most pressure, it tells me the market continues to pay a premium for GGAL compared to the rest of the system. That premium is not magic, but scale, digitization, diversification, and the ability to absorb shocks.

In addition, I think it is a reflection of an idea that I keep coming back to through my analysis, and that is that GGAL is the bank that most represents the financial history of the country. And as I already mentioned, that the bank moves with the macro does not imply that it is better or worse, but it implies that it operates in the center of the system.

Valuation: multiples that do not alarm

When I go to analyze the valuation, the first thing I see is that it is not stretched. As we see in the following table, GGAL operates around 8.27x P/E GAAP TTM, about 1.68x P/S TTM, 4.23x P/CF TTM and 1.37x P/B TTM, multiples that are low for a large bank, even considering a negative quarter.

When I go to the forecasts, I see that the P/E FWD rises to 16.73x, but that does not say as much about Galicia as about the mechanics of the indicator, because any quarter with a loss inflates the FWD. That is why I do not use it as an argument for evaluation, but I incorporate it to get a more complete view.

The rest of the TTM metrics confirm that, despite Q3, the bank’s fundamentals remain intact, as it operates with high historical profitability, competitive efficiency, margins that fell due to external shocks, and an operating capacity that was not eroded.

The risks I still see, even though the quarter is already behind

When I start listing the risks faced by GGAL, the first thing I see is that they do not appear as an ordered list, but change according to the moment of the Argentine cycle. That’s why there are signals I think we need to follow closely.

One of those risks is regulatory, as the Q3 results showed us how an adjustment in reserves or rates can reduce margins in a matter of days. Although it is not a novelty for anyone who follows Argentine banks, we must remember that the financial margin coexists with a volatility that does not depend on the bank.

Another risk I note is asset quality. I can see this from seeing that the credit irregularity has progressed, and, in a context where activity is not yet fully established, any further deterioration weighs more. While I don’t think it’s a structural problem, it is a condition that forces GGAL to handle credit growth more carefully.

And then there is the more difficult risk to measure, which affects all Argentine assets: the Argentine risk. When the political climate changes, country risk reacts (very) strongly, and GGAL reflects this almost in real time.

Although none of this changes the thesis, it shows us the environment in which Galicia has always operated, and this is what makes me a little more cautious than I would be in a macro context like the current one.

Conclusion: Maintain the same conviction with which I look at the Argentine cycle

When I reread the entire Q3, it is clear to me that it was a bad quarter, but when I contrast it with what the bank is in terms of ROE, scale, valuation, and activity, the interpretation changes completely. The quarter does not define the bank; it defines the context.

And when I look at how the market reacted, I see that it came to the same conclusion. It did not punish the balance sheet, but moved behind the macro signal that came immediately after the elections. As I already mentioned, GGAL remains the most sensitive bank to any climate change, and also one of the best vehicles for capturing any normalization, even partial.

That’s why my final position is to hold. Hold with positive bias, without exaggerations or dramatics, and without loading on this quarter more weight than it deserves. GGAL had a hit, but it was already behind, just as the macro that pressed its numbers was behind. And in a country where financial histories change from month to month, that’s worth more than a one-time result.

data.title: Zeta: Waiting For The ARPU Growth Reaccelerating
data.description:
We previously covered Zeta Global (ZETA) more than 3 months ago. Since then, the company's stock has fallen 3%, while the S&P 500 has risen 6%. However, the stock is still 50% above the level at which it was trading when we first published our report on the company on April 6, 2025, outperforming the index by more than 15 percentage points. We believe that the latest report confirms our key theses, even as ARPU growth has slowed.

Let’s move on to quarterly earnings

- Revenue jumped by 26% y/y to $337.2 mln, which exceeded consensus forecasts by almost 3%

- Adjusted EBITDA surged by 51% y/y to $71.6 mln. To clarify, our EBITDA calculations slightly differ from Zeta’s method, with a discrepancy of ~5% to the downside (our figures are a bit lower). We calculate adjusted EBITDA as operating income + D&A + stock-based compensation.

Operating metrics

The number of customers with an ARR of more than $100,000 climbed by 20% y/y to 572. ARPU for these customers totaled $579k (+4% y/y) — the slowest growth in Zeta's public history that we have tracked since 2021 and a sharp slowdown after ~11%-12% in 1Q and 2Q.

Management’s guidance

The guidance for 2025 has been raised. Revenue is set to reach $1.27-$1.28 bln, up from $1.26-$1.27 bln before. Adjusted EBITDA is poised to total $273-$274 mln, up from $264-266 mln before, which compares with our forecast of $261 mln.

There’s an initial outlook for 2026. Revenue is set to total $1.54 bln, with adjusted EBITDA pegged at $354 mln.

Our thoughts on the earnings release

The market reacted positively to the earnings report, with Zeta's shares gaining more than 10% on the same day and almost fully snapping back from October's drawdown. But then Zeta's shares fell back. We think that initial positive reaction was due to the earnings coming in above consensus, while the guidance was raised yet again. This is the 17th straight quarter followed by a better-than-expected earnings report and increased guidance.

However, when stacked against our expectations, the earnings represent a mixed bag. On the one hand, EBITDA is surging, and management continues to steadily raise its guidance. This is positive.

On the other hand, the sharp slowdown in revenue growth from 35%-36% previously to 26% in the past quarter came as an unpleasant surprise. Furthermore, ARPU momentum collapsed to low single digits. Here are the potential reasons for this:

- Political revenue in 3Q 2024. Then political advertising inflated earnings. Excluding political revenue, scaled ARPU expanded by 13% y/y in 3Q 2025. We expected that the lost political revenue would be mitigated by an increase in the proportion of agencies among customers. But this did not happen.

- Onboarding new, less mature customers. In the earnings slides we can see that the longer customers use Zeta's platform, the more they spend. Thus we can assume that newer customers deliver lower ARPU.

Given that Zeta in its slide presentation maintained its revenue retention forecast (110%-115% in the long term) and ARPU CAGR (12%-16%) and did not provide detailed comments on the declining momentum, we believe that this slowdown is likely a one-off.

In terms of expenses, one of the reasons why EBITDA rose in line with expectations despite lower revenue is that S&M (marketing) as well as overall SG&A costs dropped as a percentage of revenue. And we believe that trend is getting more sustainable, as the management talked about increased brand awareness. And he mentioned the unexpectedly high efficiency of the sales department. We would interpret that as the business is growing so fast that the pace of hiring more salespeople can barely keep up. This is a good sign.

Product development

There have been some product innovations. Most notably, at the Zeta Live conference, the company unveiled Athena, a new AI interface dubbed a conversational “superintelligent” agent that will become “the intelligent operating system for customers’ lives and businesses.”

Natively integrated into the Zeta Marketing Platform, Athena would reshape it into a voice-operated system capable of planning, starting, and analyzing marketing campaigns in real time using natural language. Currently in beta testing, it is expected to launch in the first half of 2026.

Also in focus is the acquisition of Marigold Enterprise Software for $325 mln. This is a portfolio of B2B products for customer data management, personalized marketing, and loyalty campaigns for large corporate clients. There is talk of a 100+ brand portfolio, but it is unclear to us how many of them overlap with Zeta's current customer base and what ARPU they yield (meaning that it is unclear how many unique scaled customers the deal will add). An impact on revenue and EBITDA could probably be seen as soon as 2026.

Summarizing the abovementioned, we would like to introduce some changes to our model following the quarterly earnings:

1) Changes have been made to the principle of modeling ARPU dynamics. We have decided to take a more conservative position and now expect ARPU growth to be at the midpoint of the revenue-retention guidance range, or at ~113% (management’s guidance is 110%-115%). Previously, we expected retention rates to be in the range of 114%-115%;

2) An adjustment has been made for the contribution of political advertising in 3Q-4Q 2024, so revenue growth expectations have been lowered for 4Q 2025.

3) Revisions have been applied to the method of computing the target price. We have increased the number of companies whose multiples we use as a reference when calculating the target multiple to better represent the fair level. We calculate Zeta’s target multiple as the average of companies with similar business characteristics (Salesforce, AppLovin, The Trade Desk, etc.).

Valuation

We have slightly lowered the forecast for adjusted EBITDA for 2025 from $261 mln (+40% y/y) to $260 mln (+42% y/y) following the revision of ARPU growth rate. However, we expect the EBITDA margin to be at 23.6% on the back of the more favorable actual dynamics of operating costs (SG&A).

The forecast for 2026 is $336.8 mln (+30% y/y), up from $320 mln prior due to a more favorable SG&A trajectory and increased management guidance.

The target price has been raised from $25.4 to $29.8 due to:

- a shift of the FTM valuation period (we evaluate Zeta’s stock based on adjusted FTM EBITDA);

- the increased 2026 adj. EBITDA forecast;

- the changes in the calculations of the target multiple, with the current number at 21.1 – up from 20 before.

The rating for the stock is BUY.

Bottom-line

Despite the slowdown in ARPU growth, the strong quarterly results and improved outlook confirm the sustainability of Zeta's business model. We maintain a positive outlook on the stock, anticipating further growth and margin expansion. We also suppose that ARPU growth will reaccelerate up to the middle of management's guidance.

data.title: S&P Global Data Center And Energy Innovation Summit: Lending And Investing
data.description:
The 2025 S&P Global Data Center & Energy Innovation Summit brought together industry experts on lending and investing to share their views on the rapidly changing landscape of the data center market and their expectations for the future. The conference provided a platform for experts to share know-how and strategies and to discuss trends in various facets of the market. While there are some isolated pockets of concern, the market as a whole looks poised for continued expansion.

The take

The data center market has seen unprecedented growth in the last two years, largely driven by AI workloads. Capital expenditure by hyperscale companies has skyrocketed, and experts expect over four times the expenditure in 2025 compared with 2024. The industry is also seeing many changes. The lack of power is making investors and lenders wary about renewables, even though companies maintain decarbonization goals. There is also a shift in the allocation of capital as ever-progressing GPUs become the linchpin of competitive performance. Risk is also evolving as data centers move into secondary and tertiary markets, where latency could become a concern in the long run. Overall, the experts agree that the explosive growth is not indicative of a bubble in data center builds, as the industry is relatively flexible and can halt builds if demand collapses. However, there are portions of the market that should remain alert to changing winds.

How have markets changed in the past two years, and what is the outlook for the future?

Equity market

For the past two years, AI has been a critical driver of share performance. Hyperscale tech stocks such as Alphabet Inc., Amazon.com Inc., Microsoft Corp. and Meta Platforms Inc. have made up the bulk of that performance over the last two years. The reason for this is tied to capex and the quantity of capital dedicated to building data centers that can accommodate AI modeling and large numbers of high-density GPU chips. Considering the numbers alone, looking back to 2019, those mega-cap tech stocks spent about $80 billion in capex in aggregate that year. This year, they are on track to spend over $365 billion. This has translated directly to the success of companies like NVIDIA Corp.

Green bonds and renewables

The demand from institutional investors globally is still strong; however, there has been a sharp decline in the green bond market in the last year. Through August, the global green bond market has declined by about 30%. This could be due to shifts in US attitudes toward renewables. While the future is uncertain, it is clear that the largest institutional investors are maintaining decarbonization goals. In addition, given the amount of capital expected to be deployed in the data center space over the next five years, there is a need to diversify funding sources and broaden the investor base. This is where green bonds can play a role.

On the other hand, this significant expansion of energy infrastructure will likely result in some of the hyperscalers deviating from their climate targets to some extent. It seems that no single technology will be able to provide the necessary power, and companies are moving toward what they can attain in the shortest time frame.

Lending

There has been a massive increase in lending. Forecasts show a threefold increase in project finance construction from 2024 to 2025. With a hyperscale tenant, it is typical to see a roughly 80% loan-to-cost ratio on the construction of the data center, although this may overstate how much is being funded by debt, as the 80% estimate covers just the cost of the data center shell. The power supply and the equipment inside the data center, including servers, are not included in this estimate. The cost of data center construction is about $15 million per megawatt. This could be, however, $50 million to $100 million per megawatt when considering the full buildout, with chips and the equipment that goes into the shell. So, the actual spending being debt-financed in the data center is quite low in relation to the full project.

Risk is a function of data center location and purpose

Location, coupled with the facility's function, is of paramount importance when assessing the risk involved with a data center buildout. The first consideration is the type of facility. AI training facilities that use GPUs have a depreciation consideration. Banks cannot provide long-term assumptions; they must take more of a five-year view in terms of value. Neoclouds tend to use a six-year depreciation on GPUs. Companies will argue that they still have legs, but banks must take a relatively punitive view on depreciated value.

As an example, there is a lot less risk of obsolescence for a cloud provider in Northern Virginia than an AI training facility in western Texas or rural North Carolina. In addition, tertiary markets, particularly more rural markets, can be seen as riskier than sites closer to population centers due to concerns about latency. While these sites can be used for AI training, it is not clear that these sites will be able to transfer information fast enough to support inferencing once that becomes the primary need. Banks generally find it unwise to invest against cash flows that are assumed after the initial maturity of the loan.

In the first six months of 2025, the insurance industry saw upward of $100 billion in insured losses, mostly from severe convective storms and wildfires. For example, Texas, which has had difficulty with extreme weather events in the past, tends to be seen as more risky than milder climates. Stability of the grid at the regional level is also a consideration. Areas where the grid is known to struggle during environmental extremes without data centers are a riskier proposition than areas with a more robust infrastructure. For example, an area that is prone to hailstorms but also heavily reliant on solar power has risk built in prior to the presence of a data center.

Does the AI market suggest a bubble?

From an investing perspective, the word "bubble" evokes the thought of a massive lack of profitability in the system. That doesn't seem to be the case when considering the leaders of the space, such as NVIDIA and the hyperscalers. There will be winners and losers, but the starting point must be considered, and we are starting from very steady footing regarding big tech. In terms of lending, issues may arise when the current construction loan needs to be refinanced. Much of what is being seen in the space is three-year interest-only debt; this is not an issue for the hyperscalers. On the other hand, for smaller tenants, it is unclear where the refinancing market will be if something goes wrong in the next five years.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Vistra: Deep Dive Shows Surprising Strength In Q3 Earnings
data.description:
Vistra (VST) is a large, integrated U.S. power company that combines retail electricity supply, wholesale generation, and a diversified fleet of power plants with natural gas, nuclear, coal, solar, and battery storage. The company serves millions of residential, commercial, and industrial customers across dozens of states, with a generation capacity of about 44,000 megawatts. Natural gas and coal production make up almost three quarters of their production, with nuclear next and growing.

VST has corrected 18% in the last two months but is still 28% higher YTD. Over 5 years, the stock has gained 8x and now has a market cap of $57.88 billion.

I think the dip is a good buying opportunity for VST. Electric power demand has a clear path for growth, and VST is a power-producing leader in the fossil fuel space and gaining strength in my favorite “cleaner” energy, nuclear power. Earnings have seen growth, with operational performance and cash generation also solid. A recent acquisition will help their ongoing operations and position them for continued growth.

Q3 Report Deceiving

Adjusted earnings earlier this month for Q3 were $1.38, marginally missing expectations, with revenue just shy of $5 billion also coming up short. Net income was $652 million, greater than the prior quarters this year, but down from the $1.83 billion in Q3 last year.

The initial glance at these numbers looks concerning for the most part. However, for a few reasons, I am not very worried and see a lot of positivity internally for VST. One reason for the drop in sales and net income was due to unrealized market gains on derivative positions, up to a $1.67 billion hit. It’s common for larger energy producers to hedge electricity and commodity exposure and see swings in these values. This is not an indication of internal performance and will rebound for VST at some point over the next few quarters.

Also, VST experienced a fire at their Martin Lake facility, which is an 815 MW producer. $155 million in cash capital expenditures have been incurred through Q3. Management notes that they anticipate recovering the majority of expenditures associated with the incident through insurance and business interruption proceeds. This will likely show up in earnings reports later next year, and the VST hopes to have the facility up and running again later this year or in Q1 2026.

Internal Strength

Ongoing operations adjusted EBITDA for the first nine months of this year is $4.17 billion, up 17% from last year’s first three quarters. Even though sales and the bottom line look light, this shows that their operational performance has been thriving.

Cash generation has also been solid, and management guidance expects continued growth here. At quarter end, VST had total liquidity of about $3.7 billion, with $602 million of that in cash or equivalents.

They noted in the call that they are on track to hit the upper half of their guidance for EBITDA and free cash flow for 2025. They also indicated 2026 initial target ranges for each, which includes a projected roughly 25-30% rise in each. Operationally, VST is running well, and that is more important for investors to take note of than a fall in sales and net income due to one-time events and derivative drops.

Capital Allocation & Industry Outlook

VST has an expected cumulative cash of $10 billion to allocate over the next several years. $3.4 billion is going to be sued for share repurchases and dividends.

The big news is that $2.6 billion is expected to be used for the growth of their recently acquired Lotus Infrastructure Partners. This addition includes 7 modern natural gas generation facilities, which will lead to around 2,600 MW of capacity. Demand for electric power is largely on the rise, and this deal gives VST greater resources and opportunity to present themselves to large hyperscalers with heavy data center demand.

VST has current projects with Amazon (AMZN) and Microsoft (MSFT), expected to begin operating in 2026. They also have an undisclosed buyer from a nuclear power plant that would supply 1,200 MW of power. This nuclear power plant in Texas has licensing through 2053, and I expect it to be a huge driver for them moving forward, especially with the remarks of the current administration proposing nuclear as the best energy alternative to fossil fuels.

Risks to Thesis

The biggest risk to VST is absolutely its valuation. The stock boasts a PE ratio of 61x, which is triple the sector median. The way I am justifying a buy at this value is based on a few things. The recent price correction, forward PE of 35x, and a PEG of 1.4, which is half of the sector median. Growth is expected, and even the current ratio is still well below the stock's three-year average.

Operations are running strong by the numbers, but as we already saw with the Martin Lake incident, this industry can be prone to accidents or outages that affect production, leading to lower sales. On a broader scale, I don’t see much risk to the industry at the moment, as demand is rising, and VST is ready on both the fossil fuel side and the clean energy side with their nuclear facility growth.

Conclusion

The initial look at Q3 earnings for VST is not great. The stock was dropping for weeks prior to the release and has continued downward since. I think it presents a great buying opportunity for an energy-producing leader in an industry that is agreed upon by most analysts as one with an extremely high-growth outlook over the next decade. There will certainly be volatility with this stock, as numbers can vary like you’ve seen on this earnings report. If you dig deeper, you can see the operational success and cash generation is there. This, along with the additional Lotus capacity, will lead to good results for VST in 2026.
